text:
Y’all thought and mobile game ads were carbon copies of one another. Take old coffee ads from the sixties and see that they make mobile game ads look unpicked in comparison.

Husband: Wow! Honey, your coffee sucks! 🤮🤢

Wife: That’s not very nice. 😢😭

Husband: the thing that’s not nice is your coffee. 😡🤬 And I am gonna go the other girls house to get a actual decent cup of coffee!🤬🤬

Wife: *now at store* MR.EMPLOYEE! MY HUSBAND SAYS MY COFEE SUCKS! 😥😰

Employee: Why don’t you try *name of brand goes here* coffee! Trust me your husband would love it! 😁😄

Husband: Wow! Honey, you coffee is amazing. 😋😋 Came we make love now? 😘😘

Narrator: try *name of brand goes here* ! For a new kind of coffee.

TLDR: Coffee ads were the mobile game ads of the sixties.

text:
@LeBonkJordan  You seem to be thinking about and addressing the human race as if we're some hive mind. People don't get married just to respond to social pressures, to make children, because the church told them to, or anything else like that. Humans are simply not that programmable whether they were "boomers" or not. Contextualizing human history as a struggle between dominant thoughts or motivations shared by whole populations is a very lazy, storybook way of understanding the world. People in the 1900s were primarily people, not robots being controlled by the oligarchy to produce children. People get married because we all have a very deep desire to be loved and understood by another person. Not just a desire, a need: It's almost as important as food in terms of health. Men in the 50s who complained about their wives still got married because they were lonely humans and they still got as much emotional and mental comfort from it. It's just much harder to draw up a psychological model for marriage as a societally enforced means of reproduction than it is as an act of human frailty. Do you understand?

Also, I can't assume this but I would wager you harbor some hipocrasy about human sexual drives and marriage. Do you believe that modern sexual culture, where we are all pairing casually, is good and healthy? If so, is that because the human impulse to pair themselves is natural? And if that's the case, then why does that not apply to marriage? Is marriage not simply a penultimate form of sexual pairing? Overall you seem to be excluding the concept of marriage from having natural causes. You aren't weighing its history against the lived motivations of its participants. You're falling into this stupid political trap where people always say that what they are doing is actually how people have always wanted to do things, and everyone before them was just being controlled by this or that. It amounts to nothing more than the assumption that you're right, and it's just a lens to be used selectively to convince you of that. You believe that marriage was a bad thing simply because you've analyzed it as a result of societal pressures, and you (might) think that sexual promiscuity is a good thing simply because you've analyzed it as a result of personal motivation. That's not an argument, that's a bias.
author: @jaxjax2011

text:
@jaxjax2011   I apologize if I gave off the impression that I think marriage is bad. That was not the intention. And since it's been brought up, I suppose I might as well  say that I don't consider sexual promiscuity to be a good thing, though I don't consider it bad either.

The pressure to maintain existing marriages exists for many reasons, but one of those reasons is closely related to why promiscuity is (still) taboo, as well as a number of other major sex-related taboos. Sociologically speaking, norms, mores, and taboos all exist for a reason, and the common thread here is what we now know as STDs.

Even before we knew what actually causes disease, we still knew (being the natural pattern-finders we are) how to recognize the correlation of "people doing x" to "people getting sick with y". Of course, like many other phenomena which we didn't have the means to explain, we attributed it to the gods – we assumed that people who get what we now call STDs must be getting punished by the gods for something related to sex, which leads to the assumption that certain sexual acts, partners, or frequencies must be "wrong", thus forming a taboo. At the same time, people saw that those who have sex with only one person (or none at all) generally did not contract these illnesses, so this became seen as the "right way", and so rituals to celebrate such pairings formed not long thereafter.

Importantly, these social norms and taboos persisted as religions came and went. The gods doing the punishment changed, but the behaviors largely stayed the same – as you observed, people are not so easily programmed or deprogrammed. Behaviors are, first and foremost, an adaptation to the short-term conditions of surrounding environment, from the deer in Yellowstone adapting to the reintroduction of wolves to crows learning to use periodically-passing cars at a stoplight to crack open nuts for them.

Our conditions changed shortly after we learned that diseases are not divine punishments for immoral sex, but are actually caused by the person-to-person spread of microscopic lifeforms, which allowed us to devise methods to specifically combat these pathogens as they are now called, and, crucially, this allowed us to alter our behavior to greatly hinder the spread of diseases, including STDs.

But that leaves us with a problem: the norms and taboos related to sex formed because of a correlation that we have now found to be faulty, and the sociological function they served (to prevent STDs) is no longer necessary. I believe what we're seeing now (though perhaps more visible during the '70s) is not exactly a loss of morality, but, rather, a period of experimentation and reprioritization of values and traditions to adapt to these new conditions. I can't reasonably claim to know the one "right way" to sort those priorities, but I believe that that ought to be up to each person as an individual and any partners they may have, not their parents or grandparents.

I personally doubt marriage is going away anytime soon, nor do I necessarily want it to, but I hope you can understand why I push back so hard against any suggestion that rising divorce rates is a sign of "moral degeneracy" or "the decline of western civilization" or similar.
author: @LeBonkJordan

text:
@LeBonkJordan  Again, I don't think the realities of history and people's lives coincide with your theorizing. It's a good thought process and I do agree with your reasoning but you have to account for the fact that what you're describing only accounts for a fraction of the reasons people get married. Most people end up in lifetime partnerships for their own personal reasons. Most people do most things for their own reasons and you can't make an exception to that in the case of marriage.

I don't think it's fair to contextualize the 'landscape' of marriage as being an after-effect of societal pressures and orthodoxy. And, by extension, I don't think that you could justly label the recent decline in marriages as being the result of social liberation. It does happen to coincide with your view and the progressive view of history, but is something so personal as marriage actually dependent on social science?

In my opinion, people aren't getting married as much these days because the culture and psychology of romance is terribly chaotic. Relationships used to be more personal and spontaneous. There were definitely cultural norms around dating, but people still mostly navigated it on the personal basis. Nowadays there is a hyper-dominant societal consciousness around dating that, while it doesn't lay out any expectations outright, still intrudes on people's navigation of romance. It's not uncommon these days to overthink how you should date: to consult the societal lens and strategize. It's almost inherent in dating apps. Politicized depictions of romance in media are super common too, given that progressives are interested in "correcting" its portrayal. People are less likely overall to throw themselves into a relationship and go with it. There's all this prospecting about it.

I think that by attempting to free people from societal expectations around romance, liberalism has actually just claimed a stake in them. And there's a proactivity to it that was not present with orthodox ideas about marriage. The lived experience of someone in a romantic relationship has more to do with what society expects now more than ever, even if you're "rejecting" it. Recitivism. It doesn't matter if, in the semantic sense, orthodoxy encouraged societal norms around marriage and liberalism discourages them. Societal norms are not what determine marriages, people own lives and thoughts and feelings are, and there's a lot of intrusion into those these days. You can't deny that there's this "we're all in this together" mentality to neo-modernism. You say that people are making their own choices now to not get married, but that's based on the assumption that they were choosing to get married before only because of pressures. If that's not the case, or to whatever extent that's not the case, there's actually another reason entirely.
I don't think that what I described is 100% of it but contemporary psychological issues with romance definitely account for a larger portion of the decline in marriage.

text:
Holy shit this comment section is a minefield. There are quite a few people bringing up war and other injustices to say that "men had it bad too" while some are just angrily attacking those who have said that. I'm digging up a dead conversation at this point, but I feel that these baseless/unnecessarily hostile statements should be addressed. 

As someone who used to be a MRA and a Christian Republican I can understand why hearing lots of people talk about "womens issues" makes it seem like nobody cares about mens issues. While I believe this to be untrue, I want to emphasize that I can understand feeling that way even if I don't think it's accurate. Hell, I used to believe that. However, citing that "men had it bad too" as a response is a whataboutism. It is denying the suffering of a group of people because the group you identify with has suffered as well. Your suffering is not inherently worse or more worthy of being treated than others. Yes, traditionally men are the one's who have gone to war and protected the ones they love. War is a terrible thing, and so is being sold into sex slavery. Again, your suffering does not invalidate others. It's a terrible argument, and seeing quite a few MRAs blurt out the same sentiment over and over again in response to women specific issues was a major reason why I abandoned the movement. There are points to be made for injustice towards men, but you cannot try and undermine women's suffering while bringing them up. Everyone deserves to be equal, which means you need to treat everyone's suffering with respect.

While not really a strong point, the ad hoc attacks coming from people on the other side of this argument are rather shit as well. A rational discussion requires respect from all involved to all involved. Throwing in insults about someone's relationship status only serves to make an already heated situation even more angry. When you attack people personally, all that will accomplish is making them feel vindicated because they pissed off their "enemy". Seriously, I know this is an angry situation, but ffs stop saying "you're an incel, I win". As someone who has been on the receiving end of that, all it does is make someone feel bitter and even more dug-in (siege mentality is a very real thing).

While I don't believe that the ad-hoc grenades or the disingenuous whataboutisms completely destroy each sides arguments, it does diminish them. There are much stronger points that I think could be made, and honestly I'd be interested in seeing them. (Unlikely since I'm necroposting but eh, a man can dream). text:
@wrkingrageaholic  The shows you cite are sitcoms where the main character's misery is played for laugh. If you read up a bit on feminist media analysis, you'd quickly discover that feminists DO indeed talk about these tropes - as an example of patriarchal ideas in media writing.

The henpecked husband isn't taken seriously because he's a joke. The writers don't expect the audience to take a character like that seriously because he's supposed to be a loser who can't "rein in" his wife. And the wife is supposed to be a depersonalized annoyance that the protagonist is saddled with, without an arc or agency of her own.

text: Seeing these old coffee ads makes me wonder how absolutely terrible coffee must've been back in the day, surely not only roasting methods were worse but in addition to that all coffee looks like it was instant or something and the only method to make it was a percolator which just recycles the already done coffee through the heat and the grounds again and again
author: @shroomyesc

text: Coffee in the USA in the 1950's was godawful mostly due to the fact that everyone used percolators, which tend to massively overheat coffee and make it insanely bitter. American "cuisine" also took a nosedive during that era due to 20yrs of economic depression and wartime food rationing -- almost everyone forgot how to cook and wanted the lazy & cheap way out (the American Way, don'tcha know) in the new Suburbia.
author: @Soufriere84

text: @Soufriere84  Technically it's the fault of the coffee itself, back then they were probably roasted way too dark to the point where bitter was the only flavor no matter how you brewed them (percolators just made it worse). Nowadays specialty light roast coffee calls for boiling/max water temp throughout the brewing process, so percolators probably work fine as long as you're in the light to medium roast area.
